1: Job’s sarcastic response — “how you have helped him who has no power!” — demolishes the friends’ practical futility. In contrast, Christ’s help to those with no power is actual: Hebrews 2:18 (“because he himself has suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted”); Hebrews 4:15 (“we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses”). The help that the friends could not supply, Christ supplies by entering the weakness himself.
2: Job’s sarcasm continues: you have saved the arm with no strength! The irony in the NT is that Christ does exactly this — not through the superior power of human counsel but through weakness: 1 Corinthians 1:25 (“the weakness of God is stronger than men”); 2 Corinthians 12:9 (“my power is made perfect in weakness”). The salvation of the arm with no strength is the specific form divine power takes in the cross.
3: Counsel to the one without wisdom — the genuine counsel that the friends claimed to offer but could not supply. 1 Corinthians 1:30: “Christ Jesus, who has become for us wisdom from God.” Colossians 2:3: “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.” The wisdom the friends lacked and could not provide, Christ is and provides: the Wisdom of God incarnate who is the answer to every wisdom question the book of Job poses.
4: Job challenges whose breath has spoken through Bildad — the question of the source of authoritative speech. John 16:13: “when the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all the truth, for he will not speak on his own authority, but whatever he hears he will speak.” The breath of God (the Spirit) that did not speak through Bildad speaks through Christ and through the Spirit he sends; the authoritative divine voice that the friends lacked was the voice of the Spirit whom Christ would pour out.
5: The Rephaim trembling under the waters — even the dead dread the divine. Matthew 8:29: the demons cry out to Jesus, “have you come here to torment us before the time?” — they know the appointed judge. Revelation 1:18: “I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades.” The Rephaim who tremble before God in Job 26:5 tremble before Christ as the one who has the keys of Hades.
6: Sheol naked before God, Abaddon uncovered — no realm outside divine visibility. Hebrews 4:13: “nothing in all creation is hidden from God’s sight; everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” Revelation 9:11 names Abaddon as the angel of the bottomless pit; Revelation 20:14: “Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire.” The naked transparency of Sheol before God is the transparency before Christ who holds authority over Death and Hades.
7: God stretching out the north over the void, hanging the earth on nothing — the cosmic sovereignty of the Creator. Colossians 1:16–17: “by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities — all things were created through him and for him. And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.” The earth hanging on nothing is held in place by Christ’s sustaining word (Hebrews 1:3: “upholding the universe by the word of his power”).
8: The waters bound in the clouds, the cloud not splitting — the divine management of creation’s physical systems. John 1:3: “all things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made.” The bound waters and the intact cloud are among the “all things” created and sustained by Christ the Word. The intricate ecological systems that Job marvels at are held together by the same one who took on flesh in the stable at Bethlehem.
9: God covering the face of his throne, spreading his cloud over it — the veiling of the divine throne. Hebrews 4:14–16: we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens — Christ Jesus; therefore approach the throne of grace with confidence. The throne veiled in cloud that the high priest could only approach once a year through layers of ritual protection is the throne that Christ has opened permanently for those in him.
10: The circle inscribed on the face of the waters as the boundary between light and darkness — the divine ordering of the created world. Genesis 1:3–4: God separating light from darkness was the first creative act. John 1:4–5: “in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.” The boundary between light and darkness that God inscribed at creation is the boundary that Christ’s resurrection permanently establishes: the darkness cannot overcome the light.
11: The pillars of heaven trembling at God’s rebuke — the cosmic structures responding to divine authority. Matthew 8:27: the disciples at the stilled storm: “what sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him?” The pillars of heaven that tremble at divine rebuke are the same forces that obey Christ’s voice when he rebukes the wind and sea. The sovereign authority over creation that Job’s hymn attributes to God is demonstrated in Christ’s miraculous authority over natural forces.
12: God stilling the sea and striking down Rahab by his understanding — the creative defeat of the chaos-monster. Revelation 21:1: “then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more.” The sea whose chaos-principle God struck down in Rahab is the sea that no longer exists in the new creation; the chaotic principle that threatened ordered existence is permanently defeated. Christ’s resurrection is the firstfruits of this new creation (1 Corinthians 15:20).
13: “By his breath the skies were cleared; his hand pierced the fleeing serpent” — the Spirit’s action and the defeat of the chaos-serpent. Genesis 3:15: the enmity between the serpent and the woman’s seed, whose heel would be struck but who would crush the serpent’s head. Colossians 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him” — the cross as the piercing of the ancient serpent. The fleeing serpent that God’s hand pierced in creation is the serpent defeated at Calvary.
14: “These are but the outer fringes of his works; how faint the whisper we hear of him!” — the epistemological humility before the vast incomprehensibility of God. 1 Corinthians 2:9: “no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him.” 1 Corinthians 13:12: “now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known.” The outer fringes that Job describes are the limit of present knowledge; the full knowledge awaits the face-to-face vision of Christ in the age to come.
